title: Emergent impedance due to antiferromagnetic domain wall dynamics
authors: Yuta Yamane, Jotaro J. Nakane, Yasufumi Araki, Jun'ichi Ieda
abstract: We theoretically investigate emergent impedance induced by domain-wall dynamics in antiferromagnets. Emergent impedance, arising from a combined action of spin-transfer torque and spinmotive force, was previously predicted and observed in spiral magnets. Here we develop a formalism for the electrical response of an antiferromagnetic domain wall under ac currents, and obtain analytical expressions for the resulting emergent impedance. We find that two dynamical modes play separate roles in the emergent impedance: Translational motion of the domain-wall center generates a contribution proportional to its velocity, analogous to that arising from the corresponding motion of a spiral magnet. Another contribution, unique to antiferromagnetic domain walls, originates from the time-dependent canting of the sublattice magnetizations localized within the moving domain wall, whose magnitude is inversely proportional to the antiferromagnetic exchange coupling constant. The competition between these two distinct contributions determines the sign and magnitude of the imaginary part of the emergent impedance at sub-resonant frequencies. Our results provide a fundamental insight into electron transport in antiferromagnets, and open avenues for novel antiferromagnet-based spintronics devices.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to understand how moving domain walls in antiferromagnets create electrical impedance. It reveals that a unique mechanism involving the tilting of sublattices contributes to this impedance, offering a way to tune electrical responses in future antiferromagnetic spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the origin and nature of emergent impedance in antiferromagnets induced by the dynamics of domain walls under AC currents.
main_result: The authors identify two distinct mechanisms for emergent impedance: one from the translational motion of the domain wall (similar to spiral magnets) and a unique contribution from the time-dependent canting of sublattice magnetizations, which is inversely proportional to the AFM exchange coupling.
method: The researchers use a perturbation expansion of the coupled Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equations up to the first order of the inverse AFM exchange coupling and apply an AC current density to derive analytical expressions for the complex impedance.
model_or_system: The model consists of an antiferromagnetic metal with two sublattices (A and B) and equal saturation magnetization, described by a 4x4 one-body Hamiltonian for conduction electrons and coupled LLG equations for the Néel and canting vectors.
key_observables: Emergent impedance (Z_omega), spinmotive force (SMF), emergent electric field, and the canting moment.
important_parameters: AFM exchange coupling (omega_E), spin-transfer torque (STT) coefficients (u, beta), pinning potential frequency (omega_pin), and Gilbert damping (alpha).
main_assumptions: Assumes spatial uniformity in the yz plane (1D walls), small Gilbert damping, and the limit where AFM exchange is dominant (|n| approx 1 and |m| much less than 1).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the model system with a driven domain wall; Figure 2 illustrates the two coupled DW modes (translational and canting).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of emergent impedance, develops a theoretical formalism using LLG equations and perturbation theory, identifies the two competing dynamical mechanisms, analyzes the frequency-dependent impedance regimes, and concludes with implications for spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
